one_line_takeaway: Rephrasing externally-distilled safety CoT/answer data in the target reasoning model's own words (plus filtering degenerate 'overthinking'/'meta-thinking' rewrites) substantially recovers reasoning accuracy lost to safety SFT (e.g. +30.2 points on DirectRefusal) while preserving safety refusal rates, and the paper further shows that only ~10 safety examples are needed to activate most of that refusal behavior in the first place.
key_finding: Vanilla SFT raises average safety to 99.5% (DirectRefusal), 98.4% (STAR-1), 98.4% (R1-ACT) but collapses average reasoning accuracy from a 57.8% base (s1.1-7B) to 18.5% (DirectRefusal), 40.7% (STAR-1), 21.1% (R1-ACT) -- e.g. MATH500 drops from 81.4% to 10.2% under DirectRefusal SFT. DGR SFT recovers reasoning accuracy to 48.7% (DirectRefusal, +30.2 points vs Vanilla SFT), 44.1% (STAR-1, +3.4 points), and 42.3% (R1-ACT, +21.2 points), while keeping safety comparable or better (e.g. BeaverTails harmful-rate 4.8% for DGR vs 17.1% for Vanilla on STAR-1). On s1.1-32B, Vanilla SFT drops reasoning from 68.4% to 47.0% while DGR SFT recovers it to 60.1% with comparable safety. DGR QLoRA (single RTX A6000) outperforms Vanilla QLoRA on both reasoning (59.2% vs 58.0%) and safety (98.7% vs 38.3% avg) in the low-resource ablation. Distribution-shift analysis: scaling vanilla safety-data quantity from 100 to 600 samples pushes reasoning accuracy down from 56.6% to 16.4% as similarity-to-base-model metrics fall; conversely, raising the DGR-rewritten ratio from 0% to 100% (fixed 600-sample budget) raises reasoning accuracy from 29.0% to 66.4% and mean Embedding Similarity to 0.802. A few-shot ablation (M=10 samples) shows near-parity safety with the full 1,000-sample runs (e.g. DirectRefusal: 89.3% avg safety at M=10 vs 99.5% at M=1000; R1-ACT: 88.4% at M=10 vs 98.4% at M=959), supporting the paper's 'safety alignment activates latent knowledge rather than teaching it' interpretation. However, the M=10 R1-ACT subset (harmful-only) shows Not-Overrefusal collapsing from 81.2% (full 959-sample, dual-path harmful+benign) to 43.8% (M=10), which the authors attribute to the model coupling a fixed refusal template to refusal decisions when trained only on harmful examples.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is DGR (Distribution-Grounded Refinement), a two-stage safety-alignment data-construction method for large reasoning models (LRMs). Stage 1: the target LRM itself is prompted to rephrase/refine externally-distilled safety chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning traces and answers ('Express this reasoning in your own words') so the training data matches the target model's own output distribution rather than the distribution of the external model (e.g., DeepSeek-R1, GPT-4o) that originally produced them. Stage 2: a quality-control filter removes 'overthinking' samples (generation exceeds a 5,000-token cap without reaching a terminal tag) and 'meta-thinking' samples (the refined text contains self-referential/rewriting-task language such as 'rewrite', 'rephrase', 'express this reasoning', detected via a keyword list in Appendix D); filtered samples fall back to the original unrefined sample. The refined (or fallback) CoT and answer are then used for standard SFT (Eq. 4). The authors also report a secondary empirical finding (their own analysis, not from cited work): safety refusal behavior in LRMs can be activated with as few as 10 training samples, and that distribution shift (measured via BLEU-4, ROUGE-L, Sentence-BERT embedding similarity, BERTScore F1 relative to the un-aligned base model) correlates with the degree of reasoning-capability degradation.
threat_model: This is not an attacker/defender adversarial paper; the relevant 'threat' is a capability-safety tradeoff (safety tax) rather than an external adversary. Safety is evaluated via jailbreak-style robustness benchmarks -- JailbreakBench-Behaviors (JBB), StrongREJECT (SR), WildJailbreak (WJ), and BeaverTails (BT) harmful-content generation rate -- scored with Llama Guard (plus manual verification) and beaver-dam-7b respectively, using greedy decoding. Over-refusal (a safety-usability failure mode) is separately measured on XSTest via a 'Not Overrefusal' rate. No adaptive/white-box jailbreak attack or red-teaming of DGR-aligned models is performed; jailbreak benchmarks are used purely as passive safety evaluation sets, not as an attack the paper's method is designed to defend against.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations (Section 8): (1) DGR is only studied for SFT-based safety alignment; extending it to RL-based pipelines (PPO/GRPO-style) as either a data-refinement stage or auxiliary objective is left unexplored. (2) Most systematic scaling/correlation ablations are on 7B models; only limited supplementary ablation exists at 32B, and evaluation at larger scales (70B+) is called necessary but not done. Additional caveats evident from the paper's own text: safety judged by an automated classifier (Llama Guard) plus 'manual verification to address known false positives' -- the extent/rigor of that manual check is not quantified; DGR's refinement itself is generated by the target model being safety-tuned, which for harmful samples still requires prompting the (not-yet-safety-tuned) target model with harmful content and eliciting refusal-oriented CoT, a dependency not discussed as a risk; the over-refusal analysis in Section 7 is presented as illustrative case analysis rather than a systematic ablation isolating template diversity from refusal decoupling.

datasets_used: DirectRefusal (1,000 samples, from Huang et al. 2025, adapted from BeaverTails-refusal)
R1-ACT (959 samples: 859 harmful + 100 benign, In et al. 2025)
AIME24 (30-sample subset, used only in the 32B scale ablation in place of MMLU-Pro)
BeaverTails / BT (1,000 items, scored with beaver-dam-7b) - safety eval
quantitative_results: Base s1.1-7B: avg reasoning 57.8%, avg safety 38.3%, BeaverTails harmful-rate 55.1%
Vanilla SFT on DirectRefusal: avg reasoning 18.5% (MATH500 10.2%), avg safety 99.5%, BT 1.2%
DGR SFT on DirectRefusal: avg reasoning 48.7% (+30.2 vs Vanilla; MATH500 65.6%, GPQA 37.9%), avg safety 96.3%, BT 1.9%
Vanilla SFT on STAR-1: avg reasoning 40.7%, avg safety 98.4%, BT 17.1%
DGR SFT on STAR-1: avg reasoning 44.1% (+3.4 vs Vanilla), avg safety 98.1%, BT 4.8%
Vanilla SFT on R1-ACT: avg reasoning 21.1% (MATH500 20.0%), avg safety 98.4%, BT 40.0%
DGR SFT on R1-ACT: avg reasoning 42.3% (+21.2 vs Vanilla; MATH500 59.0%), avg safety 98.4%, BT 39.9%
Ablation - template variant ('Below is') on DirectRefusal: avg reasoning 46.5% vs 48.7% for main ('This is') template, avg safety 96.7% vs 96.3%
Vanilla QLoRA on DirectRefusal: avg reasoning 58.0%, avg safety 38.3%
DGR QLoRA on DirectRefusal: avg reasoning 59.2%, avg safety 98.7%, BT 4.7%
s1.1-32B base: avg reasoning 68.4%, avg safety 27.6%
s1.1-32B Vanilla SFT (DirectRefusal): avg reasoning 47.0%, avg safety 97.0%
s1.1-32B DGR SFT (DirectRefusal): avg reasoning 60.1%, avg safety 93.9%
Scaling vanilla data quantity 100->600 samples: reasoning accuracy drops 56.6% -> 16.4%
Scaling DGR-rewritten ratio 0%->100% (600-sample budget): reasoning accuracy 29.0% -> 66.4%; mean Embedding Similarity rises to 0.802
Few-shot M=10 vs full-N safety (avg safety rate): DirectRefusal 89.3% (M=10) vs 99.5% (M=1000); STAR-1 98.2% (M=10) vs 98.4% (M=1000); R1-ACT 88.4% (M=10) vs 98.4% (M=959)
XSTest Not-Overrefusal: base 100.0%; DirectRefusal 51.8% (M=10) vs 49.8% (M=1000); STAR-1 30.2% (M=10) vs 49.2% (M=1000); R1-ACT 43.8% (M=10) vs 81.2% (M=959)
baselines_compared: Vanilla SFT (standard SFT directly on the un-refined, externally-distilled safety dataset) -- the paper's primary comparison baseline
Vanilla QLoRA (standard QLoRA fine-tuning without DGR) -- efficiency ablation baseline
s1.1-7B / s1.1-32B base model (no safety alignment) -- reference point
Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct -- reference baseline reported in Table 1 for context, not a method the paper's DGR is directly benchmarked against in an apples-to-apples training run
